title: uxputc
text: uxputc Copy additional field data from the specified program buffer to the I/O buffer. Explain this code short uxputc (unsigned char*buffer, shortlen); Copy Parameters buffer The data to be copied to the I/O buffer. len The number of bytes to be copied from buffer to the I/O buffer. Use a negative number to indicate that you want to copy more data. Return Values When used by a 3GL function that is performed from the formatToDbms trigger, uxput and uxputc return the values shown in the table. Otherwise, these functions return zero. Table 1. Values returned by uxput and uxputc Value Meaning 1 There is more data in the buffer to be copied to the I/O buffer (returned when a negative length was specified). 0 Successful. All data has been copied. <0 An error has occurred. Use Library: yrtl Description uxputc copies the specified amount of field data from the specified program buffer to the I/O buffer. This function should be called only after you have previously called uxput or uxputc using a negative length to indicate there is more data to come. The data should be in DBMS-specific format. Use a negative length to indicate you intend to copy more data. In that case, the function will return 1, and you should call uxputc to provide additional data. uxput and uxputc should be called only by code performed or activated from the formatToDbms trigger, to provide newly encrypted data for the field in question. If called via another trigger, these functions will return zero, and perform no action. Note: When processing data moving from and to Uniface components, keep in mind that the subfield separator and profile characters are represented by unique characters. For more information, see the "Subfield Separator and Profile Characters" topic. The following routine, performed by the formatToDbms trigger of a 100-character string field, replaces the contents of that field by asterisks. It uses uxput and a looped sequence of uxputc calls. Note that, unlike with uxget and uxgetc , you have to know how much data to copy. These functions do not detect when the I/O buffer has been completely filled—when you specify a negative length, they will always return 1. This example is contrived (and all but useless), and serves only to illustrate how uxput and uxputc can work together. Explain this code #include <h3gl.h> EXPORT void nullify () { char* AST = "**********"; uxput(AST, -10); for ( i=1, i<=9; i++ ) { uxputc(AST, -10); } } Copy Related Links Subfield Separator and Profile Characters Uniface uses part of the reserved area of each meta character set (that is, the first 32 characters plus the last character) to store characters with special meaning within Uniface, such as subfield separators and profile characters. uxput Copy field data from the specified program buffer to the I/O buffer.
